title: Guitar (Violão) Roadmap
shortDescription: A comprehensive roadmap to learn classical and acoustic guitar from beginner to advanced.
longDescription: This roadmap covers both the classical/erudito and popular Brazilian traditions of the violão, plus folk/popular fingerstyle and lead playing. It starts with absolute basics (parts of the guitar, posture, tuning), progresses through open chords, strumming, fingerstyle, barre chords, scales, and lead playing, then specializes into Brazilian styles (bossa, samba, MPB, choro), classical technique and repertoire (Carcassi, Sor, Tárrega, Bach, Villa-Lobos, Barrios), advanced techniques (tremolo, rasgueado), and improvisation. Ear training, sight-reading, and memorization run as parallel tracks. Free online resources draw on JustinGuitar (universally praised beginner resource), IMSLP (public-domain classical sheet music), and musictheory.net.

sections.title: Getting Started
sections.shortDescription: Anatomy, posture, hand position, and tuning.
sections.longDescription: Before you make any music, your physical relationship to the guitar matters. This section establishes the foundations: parts of the instrument, sitting and holding positions, hand basics, and how to tune.

topics.title: Parts of the Guitar
topics.shortDescription: The anatomy of nylon-string and steel-string acoustic guitars.
topics.longDescription: Knowing the parts of your instrument is the first step. You'll learn the names of the headstock, tuning pegs, nut, neck, fretboard, frets, soundhole, bridge, saddle, and strings, and understand the basic differences between nylon-string (classical/violão) and steel-string acoustic guitars.
objectives: Name all major parts of the guitar
Understand the differences between nylon-string and steel-string guitars
Identify string numbering (1 = thinnest/highest, 6 = thickest/lowest)
commonMistakes: Confusing string numbering — string 1 is the thinnest (highest pitched), not the thickest
Assuming all acoustic guitars are the same: nylon vs steel makes a big difference in technique and sound
practiceTips: Spend a few minutes physically pointing to each part as you name it

options.optionText: The string closest to your face

questions.explanation: Conventionally, string 1 is the thinnest and highest-pitched (typically tuned to E).

topics.title: Posture and Holding the Guitar
topics.shortDescription: Classical and popular sitting positions, plus standing with a strap.
topics.longDescription: There are two main sitting positions: the classical position (guitar on the left leg, often with a footstool, neck angled upward) and the popular position (guitar on the right leg, more casual). Each has implications for technique. Standing requires a properly adjusted strap.
objectives: Sit in classical position with the guitar stable and the neck at the correct angle
Sit in popular position comfortably
Adjust a strap for standing play
commonMistakes: Slouching over the guitar to see the fretboard — keep the back straight and tilt the guitar slightly toward you
Resting the picking hand wrist heavily on the guitar (introduces tension)
practiceTips: Take a tension scan every few minutes — shoulders, neck, wrists, back

questions.questionText: In classical guitar position, the guitar is typically held on which leg?

options.optionText: The left leg, often with a footstool

questions.explanation: Classical position places the guitar on the left leg (for right-handed players), often with a footstool raising the leg, so the neck angles upward.

topics.title: Right and Left Hand Basics
topics.shortDescription: Initial hand position, finger naming, and the role of each hand.
topics.longDescription: The left hand frets notes; the right hand plucks or strums the strings. (For left-handed players, the roles are reversed.) Left-hand fingers are numbered 1 (index) through 4 (pinky). Right-hand fingers use Spanish letters: p (thumb), i (index), m (middle), a (ring).
objectives: Identify left-hand finger numbers (1-4)
Identify right-hand finger letters (p-i-m-a)
Position both hands correctly at the guitar
commonMistakes: Pressing too hard on the strings (causes pain and bad tone)
Letting the left thumb wrap too far around the neck for early chord work
practiceTips: The left thumb should stay roughly behind the neck — not gripping like a baseball bat

questions.questionText: In right-hand notation for classical and fingerstyle guitar, the letter 'p' refers to:
questions.explanation: The right-hand fingers are labeled in Spanish/Latin: p (pulgar = thumb), i (índice = index), m (medio = middle), a (anular = ring).

topics.title: Tuning the Guitar
topics.shortDescription: Standard tuning (E-A-D-G-B-E) and how to tune by ear, with an app, or with a tuner.
topics.longDescription: Standard guitar tuning from low (6th string) to high (1st string) is E-A-D-G-B-E. You'll learn to use an electronic tuner or app, and also to tune relatively (matching each string to the one above it).
objectives: Tune the guitar to standard tuning using an electronic tuner or app
Tune relatively by matching the 5th fret of one string to the open string below
Recognize the names of the open strings (E-A-D-G-B-E)
commonMistakes: Tuning a string upward past the target — always come up to pitch from below
Forgetting that the 3rd string (G) to 2nd string (B) interval is a major 3rd (matched at the 4th fret, not 5th)
practiceTips: Tune every time you play — even good guitars drift
An old mnemonic for the strings: 'Eddie Ate Dynamite, Good Bye Eddie'

questions.questionText: Standard guitar tuning from the 6th string (lowest) to the 1st string (highest) is:
questions.explanation: Standard tuning is E-A-D-G-B-E, low to high.

questions.questionText: Which pair of strings is a major 3rd apart (not a perfect 4th like the others)?
questions.explanation: All adjacent string pairs are a perfect 4th apart except G to B, which is a major 3rd. That's why the 3rd string tunes to the 4th fret of the 2nd string, not the 5th.

sections.title: Reading and Counting at the Guitar
sections.shortDescription: Chord diagrams, TAB, standard notation, and rhythmic counting.
sections.longDescription: Before you can learn songs efficiently, you need to read the formats that guitar music uses: chord diagrams, TAB, and standard notation. This section covers all three plus the rhythmic counting foundation.

topics.title: Reading Chord Diagrams
topics.shortDescription: The grid-style chord chart used throughout guitar music.
topics.longDescription: Chord diagrams represent the neck of the guitar with vertical lines (strings) and horizontal lines (frets). Dots indicate where to press; numbers show fingering; 'x' means don't play that string; 'o' means play it open.
objectives: Read a standard chord diagram
Identify open strings, muted strings, and fretted positions
Recognize finger numbers in chord diagrams
commonMistakes: Reading the diagram upside down (the thick string is on the left when held in the standard orientation)

resources.title: How to Read Chord Charts (JustinGuitar, free)

questions.questionText: On a chord diagram, an 'x' above a string means:

options.optionText: Mute or don't play that string
Play that string with the thumb

questions.explanation: An 'x' above a string in a chord diagram means that string should not be played (muted or skipped).

topics.title: Reading TAB
topics.shortDescription: Guitar tablature: six horizontal lines representing strings, numbers representing frets.
topics.longDescription: TAB (tablature) is the most common guitar-specific notation. Six horizontal lines represent the six strings (the top line is the 1st/highest string). Numbers on the lines indicate which fret to press. Numbers stacked vertically mean play those notes simultaneously.
objectives: Read single-note TAB lines
Read chord shapes in TAB
Understand that TAB does not typically convey rhythm without additional rhythmic notation
commonMistakes: Reading TAB as if the bottom line is the highest string (it's actually the lowest, 6th string)
Relying only on TAB and never learning standard notation — TAB is convenient but limited
practiceTips: Compare TAB and standard notation for the same passage to see the difference

questions.questionText: In standard guitar TAB, the top line represents:
questions.explanation: Conventionally, the top line of TAB is the 1st string (highest pitch), and the bottom line is the 6th string (lowest pitch).

topics.title: Standard Notation for Guitar
topics.shortDescription: Reading staff notation as written for guitar (treble clef, sounding an octave below).
topics.longDescription: Guitar music in standard notation uses the treble clef, but sounds one octave lower than written. You'll learn the natural notes on each string in first position and how to read simple single-line passages.
objectives: Read natural notes in first position on all six strings
Understand that guitar sounds an octave below written pitch
Combine standard notation with TAB when both are provided
commonMistakes: Avoiding standard notation entirely — it limits the repertoire you can access
practiceTips: Start with just one string at a time — master each string's first-position notes before combining

resources.title: The Staff, Clefs, and Ledger Lines (musictheory.net)

questions.questionText: Guitar music in standard notation is written in:

options.optionText: Treble clef, sounding an octave below written

questions.explanation: Guitar uses treble clef but sounds one octave lower than the written pitch — this is why a sometimes-seen '8' below the clef indicates the actual sounding pitch.

topics.title: Counting Rhythm at the Guitar
topics.shortDescription: Maintain a steady pulse and count subdivisions while playing.
topics.longDescription: Rhythm is half of music. You'll practice counting basic rhythms aloud while strumming or picking, using a metronome from the start. Quarter notes, eighth notes, and basic rests are the foundation.
objectives: Count rhythms aloud while playing simple patterns
Use a metronome consistently
Maintain a steady pulse through chord changes
commonMistakes: Speeding up when something feels easy and slowing for hard sections — the metronome reveals this immediately
practiceTips: Tap your foot in time with the beat — physical engagement reinforces internal pulse

questions.questionText: Why use a metronome from the start?

options.optionText: It plays the chords for you
It enforces a steady pulse and reveals timing problems
It's only useful for advanced players

questions.explanation: A metronome provides an objective steady reference. Without it, players unknowingly speed up at easy parts and slow at hard parts.

sections.title: First Open Chords
sections.shortDescription: The first chords every guitarist learns — the gateway to hundreds of songs.
sections.longDescription: Open chords use one or more open (unfretted) strings and are played in the first few frets. This section covers the most common open chords (E, A, D, C, G in major and minor forms) and combines them into your first chord progressions.

topics.title: E minor and E major
topics.shortDescription: The two easiest beginner chords — only two fingers each.
topics.longDescription: E minor uses just two fingers on the 5th and 4th strings (2nd fret). E major adds one more finger on the 3rd string (1st fret). Both ring through all six strings, making them powerful and easy.
objectives: Play E minor cleanly with all six strings ringing
Play E major cleanly
Switch between E minor and E major smoothly
commonMistakes: Pressing too far from the fret (causes buzzing) or on top of the fret (mutes the note)
Curling the fingers so a finger touches an adjacent string and mutes it
practiceTips: Press the string just behind the fret, not in the middle of the fret space
Check each string individually after forming the chord — pick each one to verify it rings clearly

assessments.title: E minor and E major Quiz

questions.questionText: Which is true about E minor and E major chords?

options.optionText: E major has one more fretted note than E minor
E minor uses more frets than E major

questions.explanation: E minor uses 2 fingers (5th and 4th strings, 2nd fret). E major adds a 3rd finger on the 3rd string (1st fret) — that's the only difference.

topics.title: A minor and A major
topics.shortDescription: Two more essential open chords using three fingers each.
topics.longDescription: A minor and A major are foundational chords. They share the same first finger placement, with the difference being on the 3rd string. Mute the 6th string when playing these chords (don't strum it).
objectives: Play A minor and A major cleanly
Mute the 6th string while strumming
Switch between A minor and A major
commonMistakes: Strumming the 6th string by accident — it makes the chord sound muddy
Cramming three fingers into one fret (they do all fit; relax the hand)
practiceTips: Use the thumb gently touching the 6th string to mute it, or just avoid strumming it

topics.title: D major and D minor
topics.shortDescription: Two open chords played on only the top 4 strings.
topics.longDescription: D chords use only the 1st through 4th strings. The 5th and 6th strings should not be played. The fingerings are similar between D major and D minor — only the position of finger 2 differs.
objectives: Play D major and D minor cleanly
Strum only the top 4 strings
Switch between D major and D minor
commonMistakes: Strumming the 5th and 6th strings (they don't belong in the chord)

topics.title: C major and G major
topics.shortDescription: Two more cornerstones of the open-chord vocabulary.
topics.longDescription: C major uses three fingers and rings on the top 5 strings. G major uses three fingers (sometimes four) and rings on all six strings. Both are essential for hundreds of songs.
objectives: Play C major cleanly with five strings ringing
Play G major cleanly with all six strings ringing
Switch between C and G (a very common change)
commonMistakes: Failing to mute the 6th string in C major
Buzzing on the high E in G major — usually a finger touching the 1st string
practiceTips: Practice the G-to-C change slowly and often — it appears in countless songs

topics.title: First Chord Progressions
topics.shortDescription: Combine open chords into short, song-like progressions.
topics.longDescription: Real music is chord progressions. You'll combine the open chords you've learned into common progressions: G-C-D, Em-Am-D, C-G-Am-F (with a simplified F if needed), and others that map to thousands of songs.
objectives: Play I-IV-V progressions in G (G-C-D) and C (C-F-G)
Play simple minor progressions (Em-Am-D)
Play one or two complete songs using only open chords
practiceTips: Choose a song you love that uses 3-4 open chords and learn to play it through — motivation matters

sections.title: Strumming Basics
sections.shortDescription: Downstrokes, common patterns, muting, and accents.
sections.longDescription: Strumming brings chords to life rhythmically. This section starts with downstrokes, progresses to up-down patterns, common strumming patterns, and adds muting and accents.

topics.title: Downstrokes and Up-Down Patterns
topics.shortDescription: The most basic strumming motion: downward across the strings.
topics.longDescription: Start with downstrokes alone, then alternate with upstrokes for eighth-note strumming. The motion should come from the wrist, not the arm. Keep the wrist relaxed and the pick (or thumb/fingers) grazing the strings rather than hitting them hard.
objectives: Strum even quarter notes with downstrokes
Alternate down-up for eighth-note strumming
Keep the wrist relaxed and the motion fluid
commonMistakes: Strumming from the elbow (too stiff)
Gripping the pick too tightly
practiceTips: Set a metronome at 60 BPM and strum a single chord for 1 minute — focus on relaxation

topics.title: Common Strumming Patterns
topics.shortDescription: Down-up-down-up variations that create groove and feel.
topics.longDescription: Beyond steady eighth notes, strumming patterns introduce skips, ties, and accents that give a song its feel. Common patterns include D-D-U-U-D-U (the 'ultimate strumming pattern'), folk rock patterns, and ballad patterns.
objectives: Play D-D-U-U-D-U fluently with chord changes
Play 2-3 other common strumming patterns
Match a strumming pattern to a song's feel
commonMistakes: Stopping the strumming hand during chord changes (creates an unmusical pause)
practiceTips: Keep the strumming hand moving in steady up-down even when not striking — strikes happen on the beats you want

topics.title: Muting and Accents
topics.shortDescription: Damp the strings for percussive effect, and emphasize specific beats.
topics.longDescription: Palm muting (steel-string) or right-hand muting (classical) reduces sustain and adds rhythmic punch. Accents emphasize specific strums to create a stronger groove. Both techniques add texture to plain strumming.
objectives: Apply palm muting on steel-string acoustic
Accent specific beats consistently
Combine muting and accents in a strumming pattern

sections.title: Smooth Chord Changes
sections.shortDescription: Techniques to change chords quickly and cleanly.
sections.longDescription: Smooth chord changes separate frustrating playing from musical playing. This section covers anchor fingers and effective practice methods for speeding up transitions.

topics.title: Anchor Finger Technique
topics.shortDescription: When two chords share a finger position, keep that finger anchored during the change.
topics.longDescription: Many common chord changes share notes. For example, the change from C to Am keeps fingers 1 and 2 in the same positions. Recognizing and using these anchor fingers makes changes faster and cleaner.
objectives: Identify common anchor fingers between chord pairs
Keep anchor fingers down during chord changes
Apply to the most common changes (G-Em, C-Am, etc.)
commonMistakes: Lifting all fingers between every chord (slow and inefficient)
practiceTips: Look at your fingers — see what's already in the right place before the change

options.optionText: A finger that always plays the same string
A finger that stays in place during a chord change because both chords use it

questions.explanation: An anchor finger stays put during a chord change because it's already in the correct position for the next chord.

topics.title: Practicing Chord Transitions
topics.shortDescription: Methods for speeding up chord changes: 1-minute changes, ghost changes, slow practice.
topics.longDescription: Smooth chord changes are mostly about brain training, not finger strength. Effective methods include the 1-minute change exercise (count how many clean changes between two chords you can do in 60 seconds), ghost changes (form the chord shape without strumming), and slow strumming with focus on the transition.
objectives: Apply the 1-minute change exercise to your weakest chord changes
Practice ghost changes (forming chords in the air)
Strum slowly enough that no transition gap exists
commonMistakes: Practicing fast and sloppy — you reinforce the sloppiness
practiceTips: Five minutes of focused 1-minute changes daily beats an hour of mindless strumming

sections.title: Fingerstyle Fundamentals
sections.shortDescription: Right-hand fingerpicking technique — the basis of classical, fingerstyle, and Brazilian playing.
sections.longDescription: Fingerstyle technique uses individual fingers rather than a pick. This section covers the p-i-m-a finger system, free and rest strokes, basic arpeggios, and Travis picking — the foundation for classical, fingerstyle folk, and Brazilian violão playing.

topics.title: Right Hand Finger Naming (p-i-m-a)
topics.shortDescription: The Spanish letter system used in fingerstyle and classical music.
topics.longDescription: In fingerstyle and classical guitar, right-hand fingers are labeled p (thumb), i (index), m (middle), a (ring). The pinky is generally not used. Each finger usually has a default string responsibility: p plays the bass strings (4, 5, 6), i plays string 3, m plays string 2, a plays string 1.
objectives: Identify and use p, i, m, a in fingerpicking patterns
Understand the default string-finger assignments

questions.questionText: In standard fingerstyle, the index finger (i) typically plays which string?
questions.explanation: The default assignment is: p (thumb) plays bass strings 4-6, i plays string 3, m plays string 2, a plays string 1.

topics.title: Free Stroke and Rest Stroke
topics.shortDescription: Two fundamental right-hand techniques used in classical and fingerstyle playing.
topics.longDescription: Free stroke (tirando) plucks a string and lets the finger move freely into the air without touching the adjacent string. Rest stroke (apoyando) plucks the string and lands the finger on the next string, producing a fuller tone. Each has its uses: free stroke for arpeggios and chords, rest stroke for melodic emphasis and scales.
objectives: Produce a clear free stroke
Produce a clear rest stroke
Choose between them based on musical context
commonMistakes: Plucking upward (catching the string and letting it twang) rather than pulling through smoothly
Tense fingers that bounce away from the strings
practiceTips: Practice both strokes on every string — they each feel different on different strings

questions.questionText: In a rest stroke (apoyando), the finger:

options.optionText: Lifts away into the air after plucking
Lands on the adjacent string after plucking
Crosses over to the next string

questions.explanation: Rest stroke (apoyando) plucks the string and rests on the adjacent (typically lower) string, producing a fuller, projecting tone.

topics.title: Basic Arpeggio Patterns
topics.shortDescription: Pluck the notes of a chord in sequence using p-i-m-a.
topics.longDescription: Arpeggio patterns are the foundation of fingerstyle. Start with simple p-i-m-a (thumb plays a bass note, then i-m-a play the top three strings). Then add variations: p-i-m-a-m-i, p-a-m-i, etc. Practice each pattern slowly and evenly.
objectives: Play p-i-m-a smoothly over a held chord
Apply the pattern to chord progressions
Practice variations like p-i-m-a-m-i
commonMistakes: Letting one finger be louder than the others
Looking at the picking hand constantly — your fingers can learn their positions by feel
practiceTips: Anchor your thumb position relative to the strings so it doesn't wander
Practice slowly — speed comes from accurate slow practice

topics.title: Travis Picking Basics
topics.shortDescription: Alternating bass thumb pattern under a melody played by the fingers.
topics.longDescription: Named after Merle Travis, this pattern uses the thumb to play a steady alternating bass (e.g., 6th string, 4th string, 5th string, 4th string) while the i, m, and a fingers play higher strings. It's the foundation of much country, folk, and fingerstyle playing.
objectives: Play steady alternating bass with the thumb alone
Add finger notes between thumb strikes
Apply Travis picking to a simple chord progression
commonMistakes: Losing the bass pattern when adding finger notes — the thumb must stay metronomic
practiceTips: Master thumb alone first. Then add fingers gradually.

sections.title: Barre Chords
sections.shortDescription: Unlock the whole neck with movable barre chord shapes.
sections.longDescription: Barre chords use the index finger to fret multiple strings, creating movable shapes that can play any major or minor chord at any position on the neck. This section conquers F, then extends to full E-shape and A-shape barre chords and the principle of movable shapes.

topics.title: The F Major Chord
topics.shortDescription: The first barre chord every guitarist struggles with — and how to conquer it.
topics.longDescription: F major is famously difficult because it requires barring the first two strings with the index finger while the other fingers form an E-major shape. You'll learn proper hand position, finger angle, and pressure to make F ring clearly.
objectives: Play a full barre F major with all six strings ringing
Use proper thumb position behind the neck for leverage
Recognize the F shape as a movable pattern
commonMistakes: Pressing harder instead of adjusting angle — most clean barres come from angle, not force
Letting the barre finger flatten across all strings symmetrically — use the edge of the finger
practiceTips: Start with a 'mini-F' (only 4 strings) before attempting the full 6-string barre
Roll the index finger slightly so the bony edge contacts the strings, not the soft flat
Build up the barre over weeks — don't expect it in one session

questions.questionText: The best way to develop a clean F barre chord is:

options.optionText: Adjust the angle of the index finger and use the bony edge

questions.explanation: Angle matters more than pressure. The bony side of the index finger contacts the strings more firmly than the flat pad, making the barre cleaner with less force.

topics.title: E-Shape Barre Chords
topics.shortDescription: Move the E major shape up the neck to play any major chord.
topics.longDescription: The E major chord (open) can be 'translated' up the neck by barring with the index finger and shaping the rest of the hand as if playing E. The chord name comes from the note on the 6th string at the barre.
objectives: Play E-shape barre chords at multiple frets (e.g., F at fret 1, G at fret 3, A at fret 5)
Identify the root note on the 6th string
Use the same shape for minor chords (E-minor shape)
practiceTips: Memorize the notes on the 6th string up to fret 12 — it unlocks all E-shape barre chords

topics.title: A-Shape Barre Chords
topics.shortDescription: Move the A major shape up the neck for another set of barre chords.
topics.longDescription: The A major chord (open) translates up the neck similarly, with the root on the 5th string. A-shape barres often substitute the ring finger as a partial barre for the three middle strings, since fitting three separate fingers into one fret is tight.
objectives: Play A-shape barre chords (with full or partial barre)
Identify the root note on the 5th string
Use both major and minor A-shape forms

topics.title: Movable Chord Shapes
topics.shortDescription: Once a shape has no open strings, you can move it anywhere on the neck.
topics.longDescription: Barre chords are the most common movable shapes, but other shapes (jazz voicings, chord fragments) can also be moved. Knowing which fret-position equals which chord requires knowing the notes on the neck — the bridge between chord knowledge and fretboard knowledge.
objectives: Recognize when a chord shape is movable
Use the root-note string to identify the chord name
Play any major or minor chord on the neck using E-shape or A-shape

sections.title: The Fretboard
sections.shortDescription: Memorize the names of notes across the neck.
sections.longDescription: Fretboard knowledge separates beginners from intermediate players. This section covers the open string names, the natural notes up each string, and octave patterns for navigation.

topics.title: Notes on the Open Strings
topics.shortDescription: Memorize the names of the six open strings.
topics.longDescription: Knowing the open strings is the entry point for fretboard knowledge: E-A-D-G-B-E from low to high. From there you can derive every other note.
objectives: Recite the open string names from low to high and high to low
Recall the notes immediately without counting
practiceTips: Use a memorable phrase like 'Eddie Ate Dynamite, Good Bye Eddie'

topics.title: Notes Up the Fretboard
topics.shortDescription: Learn the natural notes on each string up to fret 12.
topics.longDescription: Each fret raises the pitch by one half step. Memorizing the natural notes (no sharps/flats) up each string is a major undertaking but transformative. Focus on the 6th and 5th strings first — they are the root strings for most barre chords.
objectives: Recall any natural note on strings 6 and 5 within 2 seconds
Extend to all six strings
Recognize that fret 12 returns to the open-string note one octave higher
commonMistakes: Trying to memorize all six strings at once — focus on one or two strings per week
practiceTips: Drill notes on the 6th string daily for a week, then 5th the next week, etc.
Test yourself before going to sleep — sleep consolidates memory

questions.questionText: What note is on the 5th fret of the 6th (low E) string?
questions.explanation: The 6th string open is E. Going up by half steps: F (fret 1), F♯ (2), G (3), G♯ (4), A (5). Five frets up from E is A.

topics.title: Octaves and Patterns
topics.shortDescription: Use octave shapes to find the same note in multiple places.
topics.longDescription: An octave shape is a fingering pattern that produces the same note an octave apart. The most common is: a note on the 6th string and a note two frets up on the 4th string. Octave shapes are powerful for fretboard navigation and improvisation.
objectives: Identify and play octave shapes between adjacent string pairs
Use octaves to find notes quickly across the neck
Apply octave shapes in melodic playing

sections.title: Scales on Guitar
sections.shortDescription: Pentatonic and full scales across the neck.
sections.longDescription: Scales unlock lead playing and improvisation. This section covers the minor and major pentatonic scales, full major and minor scales, and the CAGED-style position system that maps scales across the entire neck.

topics.title: Minor Pentatonic Scale
topics.shortDescription: The five-note scale at the heart of rock, blues, and many pop solos.
topics.longDescription: The minor pentatonic scale has five notes per octave (root, ♭3, 4, 5, ♭7). It's typically learned in five 'positions' across the neck. Position 1 (around the root on the 6th string) is the most common starting point.
objectives: Play position 1 of the minor pentatonic in any key
Recognize the scale pattern visually on the neck
Apply the scale to simple melodic phrases
commonMistakes: Playing the scale endlessly up and down — practice in musical phrases instead
practiceTips: Hum a melody while playing the scale — make sure you can hear the notes as music, not as a mechanical pattern

questions.questionText: How many distinct notes are in a pentatonic scale per octave?
questions.explanation: 'Penta' = five. A pentatonic scale has five distinct notes per octave.

topics.title: Major Pentatonic Scale
topics.shortDescription: The major-sounding cousin of the minor pentatonic.
topics.longDescription: The major pentatonic uses notes 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 of the major scale. It has the same fingering shape as the minor pentatonic — just played starting on a different note (three frets lower for the major form).
objectives: Play position 1 of the major pentatonic in any key
Understand the relationship to the minor pentatonic (same notes, different root)
Apply to country, folk, and bright-sounding genres

topics.title: Natural Minor and Major Scales
topics.shortDescription: The seven-note scales that underlie most Western music.
topics.longDescription: Beyond pentatonics, the full major and minor scales add two more notes per octave. Each scale has multiple positions on the neck. Begin with the most common positions and gradually connect them.
objectives: Play one position of the major scale in any key
Play one position of the natural minor scale in any key
Begin connecting adjacent positions
practiceTips: Practice scales in a musical context — over a chord progression in the same key — not as isolated drills

topics.title: Scale Positions and Connecting Them
topics.shortDescription: Five positions per scale, played across the neck and connected smoothly.
topics.longDescription: Each scale has five (CAGED) positions across the neck. Mastering all five and the transitions between them is the basis for fluent lead playing across the whole fretboard. This is a long-term project.
objectives: Play all five positions of the minor pentatonic across the neck
Slide between adjacent positions
Improvise using multiple positions
practiceTips: Take one position per week — patient, deliberate practice beats trying to learn everything at once

sections.title: Lead Playing Basics
sections.shortDescription: Single-note melodies, hammer-ons, pull-offs, bends, and vibrato.
sections.longDescription: Lead playing emphasizes single-note melodic and improvisational playing. This section covers single-note melodies, the legato techniques of hammer-ons and pull-offs, and the expressive techniques of bending and vibrato.

topics.title: Single-Note Melodies
topics.shortDescription: Play melodies one note at a time rather than as chords.
topics.longDescription: Melodies on guitar can be played on a single string, across strings within a position, or moving up and down the neck. Start with simple, well-known melodies (folk songs, nursery rhymes) and apply correct fingering.
objectives: Play simple melodies in first position
Use alternating finger or pick technique for fluency
Maintain a steady pulse
commonMistakes: Using only one finger for melodies (slow and inefficient)
practiceTips: Sing the melody while you play it — confirms your ear is engaged

topics.title: Hammer-Ons and Pull-Offs
topics.shortDescription: Sound a note by tapping or pulling the fretting finger rather than picking.
topics.longDescription: A hammer-on sounds a higher note by hammering a fretting finger onto the string after a previously picked note. A pull-off sounds a lower note by pulling the fretting finger off. Together they create the legato sound common in rock, blues, and Celtic music.
objectives: Execute clean hammer-ons across the neck
Execute clean pull-offs
Combine them in melodic phrases
commonMistakes: Weak hammer-ons that don't sound clearly — hammer firmly and quickly
Pulling off straight down instead of slightly outward — the pull-off needs a small sideways flick

topics.title: String Bending
topics.shortDescription: Bend a string upward to raise the pitch.
topics.longDescription: Bending pushes a string sideways across the fretboard, raising its pitch. Common bends are a half step, whole step, or one and a half steps. It's a staple of blues, rock, and country lead playing — more typical on steel-string acoustic and electric than on nylon-string classical.
objectives: Bend a string accurately to a specific target pitch
Support the bend with multiple fingers for control
Recognize and execute notated bends in TAB
commonMistakes: Bending without listening — bend to a pitch, not just a distance
Using one finger alone — multiple fingers behind the bending finger provide leverage

topics.title: Vibrato
topics.shortDescription: Add expressive pitch variation to a sustained note.
topics.longDescription: Vibrato is small, controlled pitch oscillation that adds expression to a note. It's produced by repeatedly bending the string slightly up and back. Different styles call for different vibrato speeds and widths.
objectives: Produce controlled vibrato on any string
Match vibrato style to the musical context
Vary speed and width consciously
commonMistakes: Uncontrolled, nervous vibrato — relax and slow down

sections.title: Brazilian Styles
sections.shortDescription: Bossa nova, samba, MPB, and choro — the heart of the Brazilian violão tradition.
sections.longDescription: Brazilian popular music has a deep tradition centered on the violão. This section introduces bossa nova accompaniment, samba on guitar, MPB harmonic vocabulary, and the foundations of choro — Brazil's first urban popular genre.

topics.title: Bossa Nova Accompaniment
topics.shortDescription: The understated, syncopated style created by João Gilberto and contemporaries.
topics.longDescription: Bossa nova accompaniment combines a steady bass played by the thumb with syncopated chord stabs played by the fingers. The classic bossa pattern ("batida de bossa") creates the genre's iconic feel. Chord vocabulary includes 7ths, 9ths, and other extended harmonies.
objectives: Play the basic bossa nova rhythmic pattern with thumb bass and finger chords
Apply extended chords (m7, maj7, 7) to bossa nova
Accompany a simple bossa tune like 'Garota de Ipanema' or 'Águas de Março'
commonMistakes: Playing the bass too loud — bossa is intimate; the bass should be present but understated
Rushing the syncopated finger pattern
practiceTips: Listen extensively to João Gilberto — his recordings define the style

questions.questionText: What characterizes the bossa nova guitar accompaniment style?

options.optionText: Syncopated chord stabs played by the fingers over a steady thumb bass

questions.explanation: Bossa nova's accompaniment style uses the thumb on bass strings (often steady) with the fingers playing syncopated chord stabs on the upper strings.

topics.title: Samba Rhythms on Guitar
topics.shortDescription: Translate samba's percussive groove to the guitar.
topics.longDescription: Samba on guitar uses techniques similar to bossa nova but with a more energetic, accented feel. The right hand may employ partial strumming, palm muting, and percussive techniques to evoke the rhythm section.
objectives: Play a basic samba accompaniment pattern
Add percussive elements (muted strums, chucks)
Distinguish samba feel from bossa nova feel

topics.title: MPB Accompaniment
topics.shortDescription: Música Popular Brasileira covers many styles — what they share is harmonic richness.
topics.longDescription: MPB (Música Popular Brasileira) encompasses a range of styles from Chico Buarque to Gilberto Gil to Caetano Veloso. The common thread on guitar is rich harmony (extended chords, voice leading), syncopated rhythms, and intimate accompaniment of song.
objectives: Apply extended chords (7, 9, 11) to MPB tunes
Use smooth voice leading between chords
Accompany a sung melody with sensitivity

topics.title: Introduction to Choro
topics.shortDescription: Brazil's first urban popular music, predating samba.
topics.longDescription: Choro emerged in 19th-century Rio de Janeiro, blending European dance forms with African rhythmic influence. The guitar (violão) plays a key role, often providing bass lines (baixaria) that connect chord changes. Choro is technically demanding and harmonically sophisticated.
objectives: Understand the role of the violão in a choro ensemble
Play basic baixaria (bass line) between chords
Recognize key choro composers: Pixinguinha, Jacob do Bandolim, Garoto
practiceTips: Listen to recordings of Pixinguinha, Jacob do Bandolim, and other choro masters

sections.title: Classical Guitar Technique
sections.shortDescription: Right-hand tone production, Giuliani arpeggios, and left-hand independence.
sections.longDescription: Classical guitar technique demands precise tone production and independent control of both hands. This section covers right-hand position and nail care, refined rest and free strokes, Giuliani's foundational arpeggio studies, and left-hand independence.

topics.title: Right Hand Position and Nail Care
topics.shortDescription: Position the right hand for clear tone, and care for the nails that shape that tone.
topics.longDescription: Classical guitar tone comes largely from the right hand. The hand position relative to the strings, the angle of finger contact, and the shape and condition of the nails all matter. You'll learn the standard position and basic nail filing.
objectives: Position the right hand correctly relative to the soundhole
File nails to a smooth, slightly curved shape
Use the nail and flesh together for warm yet clear tone
commonMistakes: Cracked or rough nails that produce a scratchy sound
Hand position too far from or too close to the bridge — affects tone color
practiceTips: Use fine-grit sandpaper or a glass file for nail finishing — the smoother the nail edge, the smoother the tone

topics.title: Detailed Rest Stroke (Apoyando)
topics.shortDescription: Refine rest stroke for melodic projection and scale playing.
topics.longDescription: Refined rest stroke produces a full, projecting tone, especially valuable for melodic lines. The finger moves through the string from the joint, lands solidly on the adjacent string, and the hand stays relaxed throughout.
objectives: Produce a strong, even rest stroke on every string
Apply rest stroke in scale passages
Alternate between i-m fingers with even tone

topics.title: Detailed Free Stroke (Tirando)
topics.shortDescription: Refine free stroke for arpeggios, chords, and polyphonic playing.
topics.longDescription: Free stroke leaves the finger free after plucking, allowing multiple fingers to play different strings without interference. Essential for arpeggios and polyphonic textures where rest stroke would damp adjacent strings.
objectives: Produce a clear free stroke that doesn't lift away unnecessarily
Apply to arpeggio patterns
Maintain even tone across i, m, a fingers

topics.title: Giuliani 120 Right Hand Studies
topics.shortDescription: The foundational right-hand arpeggio studies of classical guitar.
topics.longDescription: Mauro Giuliani's Op. 1 contains 120 arpeggio patterns over a simple two-chord progression (C-G7-C). Working through them systematically builds right-hand fluency unmatched by any other exercise.
objectives: Work through Giuliani's 120 patterns systematically
Maintain even tone and tempo across all patterns
Use these as warm-ups indefinitely
practiceTips: Pick 3-5 patterns per session — not all 120 at once

resources.title: Giuliani - Op. 1 Studio per la Chitarra (IMSLP, free)

topics.title: Left Hand Independence and Fingerings
topics.shortDescription: Refine left-hand technique for classical demands: polyphony, planted fingers, slurs.
topics.longDescription: Classical guitar often requires multiple voices played simultaneously, meaning left-hand fingers must operate independently. Planting fingers in advance (anticipation), using minimal motion, and accurate slur execution (hammer-ons, pull-offs) are core skills.
objectives: Plant fingers in advance to prepare upcoming notes
Use minimal finger motion (raised fingers waste energy and time)
Execute clean slurs (legato) within classical repertoire

sections.title: Intermediate Repertoire
sections.shortDescription: Carcassi, Sor, Tárrega, Brazilian standards, and fingerstyle arrangements.
sections.longDescription: This section brings the techniques together in actual repertoire: 19th-century classical studies (Carcassi, Sor), short Tárrega pieces, Brazilian bossa nova and choro, and popular fingerstyle arrangements.

topics.title: Carcassi and Sor Studies
topics.shortDescription: 19th-century classical guitar studies that remain core intermediate repertoire.
topics.longDescription: Matteo Carcassi's 25 Études Mélodiques Op. 60 and Fernando Sor's various études (Op. 6, Op. 31, Op. 35) are the bread and butter of intermediate classical guitar repertoire. They develop technique within musical contexts.
objectives: Learn at least 3 Carcassi études from Op. 60
Learn at least 3 Sor études
Apply classical technique (rest stroke, free stroke, accurate fingerings)

topics.title: Tárrega Pieces
topics.shortDescription: Francisco Tárrega's accessible classical guitar miniatures.
topics.longDescription: Francisco Tárrega (1852-1909) wrote some of the most beloved classical guitar pieces, including 'Lágrima', 'Adelita', and the famous 'Recuerdos de la Alhambra' (which is more advanced because of its tremolo technique). His shorter pieces are excellent intermediate repertoire.
objectives: Learn 'Lágrima' and 'Adelita'
Work on 'Estudio en Mi Menor' and other short pieces
Begin preparation for 'Recuerdos de la Alhambra' (requires tremolo, covered later)

topics.title: Intermediate Brazilian Repertoire
topics.shortDescription: Bossa nova standards and accessible choros.
topics.longDescription: Intermediate Brazilian repertoire includes accessible bossa nova standards ('Garota de Ipanema', 'Corcovado', 'Wave'), simpler choros, and MPB arrangements. Most of this repertoire is under copyright, so resources are typically commercial.
objectives: Accompany 2-3 bossa nova standards confidently
Play through 1-2 simpler choros
Arrange a popular MPB song for solo guitar
practiceTips: Recordings are the primary teaching resource — listen extensively

topics.title: Popular and Fingerstyle Arrangements
topics.shortDescription: Solo guitar arrangements of popular songs, plus fingerstyle originals.
topics.longDescription: Solo fingerstyle arrangements combine melody, harmony, and bass on the guitar alone — the same approach used in classical playing applied to popular tunes. Many arrangements of folk, pop, and film music are accessible at the intermediate level.
objectives: Learn 2-3 fingerstyle arrangements of songs you enjoy
Apply classical technique to popular repertoire
Develop arranging instincts: where to place melody, when to add bass, what to omit

sections.title: Advanced Technique
sections.shortDescription: Tremolo, rasgueado, tapping, and modern fingerstyle techniques.
sections.longDescription: This section covers advanced techniques specific to various styles: classical tremolo, flamenco rasgueado, two-hand tapping, and modern fingerstyle integrations of percussion and harmonics.

topics.title: Tremolo
topics.shortDescription: Rapid alternation of fingers on a single string creating a sustained, vocal effect.
topics.longDescription: Classical guitar tremolo uses the pattern p-a-m-i (thumb on a bass note, then ring-middle-index in rapid succession on a melody string). The goal is evenness — every note should sound the same in volume and timing. Tárrega's 'Recuerdos de la Alhambra' is the iconic example.
objectives: Produce even tremolo at slow tempo
Gradually increase speed while maintaining evenness
Apply to 'Recuerdos de la Alhambra' or other tremolo studies
commonMistakes: Uneven fingers — one finger louder or faster than the others
Speeding up before evenness is established
practiceTips: Practice with a metronome at slow tempo for weeks
Each finger should sound identical to the others — record yourself to verify

topics.title: Rasgueado
topics.shortDescription: The flamenco strumming technique using rolling fingers.
topics.longDescription: Rasgueado strums the strings by flicking individual fingers outward from a closed fist position. It produces a powerful, rolling sound characteristic of flamenco. Many variations exist (e and a, four-finger, five-finger rolls).
objectives: Execute basic four-finger and five-finger rasgueado
Apply to flamenco-inspired rhythms
Maintain hand and arm relaxation during fast rasgueado

topics.title: Tapping
topics.shortDescription: Use the picking hand to tap notes on the fretboard for fast, wide-interval passages.
topics.longDescription: Tapping uses fingers of the picking hand to hammer notes on the fretboard, in combination with the fretting hand. It enables fast, wide-interval lines impossible with conventional technique. More common on electric guitar but applicable to acoustic.
objectives: Execute basic two-hand tapping patterns
Combine tapping with hammer-ons and pull-offs
Apply to passages in lead playing

topics.title: Advanced Fingerstyle Techniques
topics.shortDescription: Percussive techniques, harmonics, and complex polyphonic playing.
topics.longDescription: Modern fingerstyle (associated with players like Michael Hedges, Tommy Emmanuel, and Andy McKee) integrates percussive hits on the guitar body, natural and artificial harmonics, alternate tunings, and elaborate polyphonic textures.
objectives: Produce clean natural harmonics on key frets (5, 7, 12)
Add basic percussive elements (taps on the body)
Explore alternate tunings (DADGAD, drop D, etc.)

sections.title: Advanced Repertoire
sections.shortDescription: Bach, Tárrega masterworks, Villa-Lobos, Barrios, and contemporary classical guitar.
sections.longDescription: Advanced repertoire opens the great solo guitar literature: Bach transcriptions, Tárrega's masterworks (including the iconic 'Recuerdos de la Alhambra'), 20th-century giants Villa-Lobos and Barrios, and contemporary composers expanding the instrument's possibilities.

topics.title: Bach on Guitar
topics.shortDescription: Lute suites, cello suites, and violin works transcribed for guitar.
topics.longDescription: J.S. Bach didn't write for guitar specifically, but his lute suites (BWV 995-1000, 1006a), the cello suites, and the violin sonatas and partitas have become essential classical guitar repertoire. These works develop polyphonic playing and musical maturity.
objectives: Learn movements from a Bach lute suite or cello suite arrangement
Master polyphonic voicing required for Bach
Develop stylistic understanding of Baroque ornamentation and phrasing
commonMistakes: Treating Bach as a virtuoso showcase rather than as polyphonic music — voice independence matters more than speed

topics.title: Tárrega Advanced Works
topics.shortDescription: 'Recuerdos de la Alhambra' and other Tárrega masterworks.
topics.longDescription: Tárrega's mature works include 'Recuerdos de la Alhambra' (the iconic tremolo study), 'Capricho Árabe', and the 'Gran Vals'. These pieces require all the advanced classical techniques and stand among the most performed solo guitar music.
objectives: Master tremolo for 'Recuerdos de la Alhambra'
Learn 'Capricho Árabe' or other advanced Tárrega works
Apply mature musical interpretation

topics.title: Villa-Lobos and Barrios
topics.shortDescription: Two great 20th-century composers whose works defined modern classical guitar.
topics.longDescription: Heitor Villa-Lobos (1887-1959, Brazilian) and Agustín Barrios Mangoré (1885-1944, Paraguayan) wrote some of the most important classical guitar music of the 20th century. Villa-Lobos's 12 Études and 5 Preludes are landmarks; Barrios's 'La Catedral' and 'Un Sueño en la Floresta' are showpieces. Note: most of these works are still under copyright in many jurisdictions.
objectives: Learn at least one Villa-Lobos Prelude or Étude
Learn at least one Barrios work
Engage with the Latin American classical guitar tradition
practiceTips: Listen to recordings by Andrés Segovia, John Williams, Julian Bream, and Brazilian masters like Sérgio Abreu and the Assad brothers

topics.title: Contemporary Composers
topics.shortDescription: Leo Brouwer, Sérgio Assad, Roland Dyens, and other modern voices.
topics.longDescription: Late 20th- and 21st-century classical guitar composers have expanded the instrument's vocabulary with new techniques, jazz influences, world music elements, and contemporary harmonic languages. Leo Brouwer (Cuban), Sérgio Assad (Brazilian), Roland Dyens (French), Carlo Domeniconi, and others have written extensively.
objectives: Learn at least one work from a contemporary composer
Engage with extended techniques specific to modern works
Develop interpretive flexibility for varied modern styles

sections.title: Improvisation and Stylistic Application
sections.shortDescription: Blues, modal, Brazilian, and jazz improvisation and comping.
sections.longDescription: Improvisation extends technique into musical creation. This section progresses from blues improvisation through modal soloing, Brazilian improvisation, and jazz comping.

topics.title: Blues Improvisation
topics.shortDescription: The 12-bar blues form and improvising over it with pentatonic and blues scales.
topics.longDescription: The 12-bar blues progression (I7-IV7-V7 in various combinations) is the most common improvising vehicle for beginners and intermediates. The minor pentatonic scale with the added ♭5 ('blue note') is the standard improvising vocabulary.
objectives: Play a 12-bar blues rhythm guitar pattern
Improvise over a 12-bar blues using minor pentatonic with the blue note
Develop phrasing: call and response, motif development
commonMistakes: Running the scale up and down without phrasing
Ignoring the chord changes — target chord tones on strong beats
practiceTips: Find a backing track and just play. Listening time matters as much as practice time.

topics.title: Modal Soloing
topics.shortDescription: Improvise using modes for jazz, rock, and fusion contexts.
topics.longDescription: Beyond major and minor scales lie the seven diatonic modes (Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, etc.). Each has a distinctive sound, and each pairs with specific chord types. Mixolydian fits over dominant 7th chords; Dorian fits over minor 7th chords; Lydian fits over major 7th chords.
objectives: Recognize which mode fits which chord type
Improvise using Dorian, Mixolydian, and Lydian in appropriate contexts
Use modes as a coloration tool, not just a scale-of-the-moment

topics.title: Brazilian Improvisation
topics.shortDescription: Soloing in bossa, choro, and samba contexts.
topics.longDescription: Improvising in Brazilian music involves recognizing the chord progressions (often with rich harmony — m7, maj7, 7b9, etc.) and using appropriate scales (often modes, melodic minor, or bebop-influenced ideas). The phrasing is conversational rather than displaying.
objectives: Improvise over a bossa nova chord progression
Choose scales/modes that fit each chord
Develop rhythmically and stylistically appropriate phrasing
practiceTips: Transcribe solos by Brazilian masters — listening time is essential

topics.title: Jazz Comping
topics.shortDescription: Provide harmonic accompaniment in a jazz context.
topics.longDescription: Comping (from 'accompany') is the art of providing rhythmic and harmonic support to a soloist. You'll learn comping chord voicings (often 'shell voicings' with just the root, 3rd, and 7th), rhythmic patterns (the classic Freddie Green 'four-on-the-floor', bossa comping, modern comping), and how to listen and respond to soloists.
objectives: Play shell voicings for common jazz chords
Apply common comping rhythms (four-on-the-floor, syncopated patterns)
Comp through a jazz standard (Rhythm Changes, ii-V-I in many keys)

sections.title: Ear Training and Memorization
sections.shortDescription: Playing by ear, transcription, and reliable memorization — parallel skills.
sections.longDescription: This section covers three skills that develop in parallel with everything else: playing by ear, transcription, and memorization for performance.

topics.title: Playing by Ear
topics.shortDescription: Reproduce melodies and chord progressions on the guitar without sheet music.
topics.longDescription: Playing by ear is fundamental for any guitarist working in popular music, where charts are often sparse or absent. Start with simple melodies, then chord progressions, then complete songs.
objectives: Work out simple melodies by ear
Identify chord progressions by ear (start with I-IV-V)
Build a repertoire of songs learned without sheet music
commonMistakes: Searching randomly — listen first, then play
Skipping this skill — it's irreplaceable for popular music
practiceTips: Start with songs you've heard many times — easier to hear when the song is in your memory
Use software to slow recordings without changing pitch

topics.title: Transcription
topics.shortDescription: Notate what you hear — a deep ear-training discipline.
topics.longDescription: Transcribing solos and arrangements from recordings is one of the most efficient ways to develop your ear, your stylistic vocabulary, and your repertoire. Choose recordings slightly above your current level and work them out note by note.
objectives: Transcribe a melody or short solo from recording
Notate the result in TAB or standard notation
Use the transcription as a springboard for your own playing
practiceTips: Slow software (e.g., Anytune, Transcribe!) makes a huge difference
Transcribe one short phrase at a time, master it, then move on

topics.title: Memorization for Performance
topics.shortDescription: Memorize pieces robustly enough to perform them with confidence.
topics.longDescription: Memorization for performance combines muscle memory (the hands know where to go), aural memory (you can hear the piece in your head), visual memory (you see the score or the fingerboard), and analytical memory (you understand the harmony and structure). All four together produce robust performance memory.
objectives: Memorize a piece using all four memory modes
Test memory by starting from arbitrary points in the piece
Maintain memorized repertoire through periodic review
commonMistakes: Relying only on muscle memory — it breaks under pressure
practiceTips: Play through pieces mentally, away from the guitar — if you can do that, memory is solid
